description:
You are acting as a DevOps engineer who has just added a tiny utility script to the codebase and now you need to capture a very small performance benchmark before opening a merge-request.  
The benchmark must be recorded in a **single CSV log file** so that the CI system can archive the numbers and let other tools graph them later.

Context that already exists on disk  
------------------------------------------------  
1. Utility script to benchmark  
   • Path       : /home/user/scripts/sleep_for.py  
   • Purpose    : Receives one positional integer argument (N) and simply sleeps for N seconds, then exits.  
2. The GNU time binary is available at /usr/bin/time and supports the “-f” option.  
3. GNU parallel is installed as the command parallel.  
4. Nothing has been benchmarked yet; no target directory for the upcoming log exists.

Your job  
------------------------------------------------  
1. Benchmark three separate invocations of /home/user/scripts/sleep_for.py with the inputs 1, 2 and 3 seconds.  
2. Run the three measurements **concurrently** (i.e. in parallel) so that the total wall-clock time for all three jobs stays close to 3 seconds instead of the sum of 6 seconds.  
3. For each invocation you must capture ONLY the “real / elapsed” running time (in seconds) as emitted by GNU time with the “%e” format specifier.  
4. Collect the data from all three runs and write them—**exactly in the order 1, 2, 3**—into the file  
   /home/user/benchmarks/sleep_benchmark.csv

Required CSV format (must be **exactly** like this)  
------------------------------------------------  
• The file must contain **one header row** followed by **three data rows**.  
• Field separator is a literal comma (no spaces).  
• Header must be exactly:  
  input_seconds,real_time_seconds  
• Each data row must look like:  

  <integer>,<floating-point number with optional decimal fraction>  

  Example (obviously with different numbers):  

  1,1.004  
  2,2.002  
  3,3.006  

5. Do not round or pad the elapsed time; just write it exactly as returned by GNU time (it usually prints with 3 decimal places in this container).  
6. Make sure /home/user/benchmarks exists before writing the file; create it if necessary with default user permissions.  
7. When everything is done you can cat the CSV to STDOUT so users can eyeball it, but that is optional.

Hints  
------------------------------------------------  
• Remember that GNU parallel can read arguments from stdin and replace them inside the command line with “{}”.  
• GNU time writes its timing information to STDERR by default; you will need to capture / redirect that stream to pick up the elapsed time.  
• Pay close attention to the order requirement; running jobs in parallel normally returns results in the same order the arguments were supplied when “–keep-order” (-k) is used.  
• The automated test will only accept a correctly-formatted CSV containing the three lines 1,… 2,… and 3,… in that order, with numeric elapsed-time values greater than zero.

Success criteria checked by the grader  
------------------------------------------------  
1. /home/user/benchmarks/sleep_benchmark.csv exists.  
2. It contains exactly 4 lines (1 header + 3 data lines).  
3. Header is precisely “input_seconds,real_time_seconds”.  
4. Data lines are in the order 1, 2, 3 and each has a positive real-time value matching the regex “^[0-9]+,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$”.  
5. Each invocation of sleep_for.py exited with code 0 (the grader will rerun them if necessary).
truth:
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pre-existing assets in the container (do NOT reveal to the agent)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1) Benchmark target script
# ------------------------------------------------
# Path : /home/user/scripts/sleep_for.py
# Contents exactly:
#     import sys, time
#     if __name__ == "__main__":
#         secs = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 0
#         time.sleep(secs)
#
# It has mode 0755 and is owned by user:user.

# 2) Directory structure and permissions
# ------------------------------------------------
# /home/user and every directory the agent must write to is already
# owned by the normal user (uid 1000).  No /home/user/benchmarks
# directory exists at container start.

# 3) What the test harness will validate
# ------------------------------------------------
# a) That the file /home/user/benchmarks/sleep_benchmark.csv exists.
# b) It must have exactly four newline-terminated lines.
# c) Line-0 (header) must be:
#       input_seconds,real_time_seconds
# d) Lines 1-3 must match, respectively, the regexes:
#       ^1,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$
#       ^2,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$
#       ^3,[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$
# e) No extra whitespace anywhere; values are separated by exactly one comma.
# f) The three elapsed-time numbers must all be ≥1.0, ≥2.0, ≥3.0
#    respectively and ≤(value+0.5) to allow for minor overhead.
# g) If any field, order, or numeric constraint fails, the test fails.

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
name: task_000000_81977510
